workflow.name: Classify LinkedIn posts as quality or slop with OpenAI and Qdrant
workflow.description:
## Purpose

This workflow is the official backend for the StopSlopIn Chrome extension – it classifies LinkedIn posts as quality or slop using a strict LLM quality gate and learns from user votes over time via a Qdrant vector store.

## What this is for

This runs the webhook that powers the [StopSlopIn Chrome extension](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/pbflmnidhehgfpcjhcplegpcfohcnbjk) on the Chrome Web Store. The extension sends LinkedIn posts here for analysis and user votes here for training – everything stays on your own n8n instance.

## Setup

- Add your OpenAI credentials to the chat model and embeddings nodes
- Add your Qdrant credentials to both vector store nodes, pointing to a collection named `stopslopin`
- Activate the workflow, copy the webhook URL, and paste it into the StopSlopIn Chrome extension settings
- Follow the instructions on the yellow sticky notes for anything else

## How it works

A single webhook exposes two actions, selected via a `?action=` query parameter: `analyze` for classification, `vote` for training.

1. A `Switch` node routes incoming requests based on the `action` parameter
2. On `analyze`: each post is enriched with similar prior-rated posts pulled from Qdrant (RAG), batched together, and sent to the LLM with a strict quality-gate system prompt
3. The LLM returns a structured JSON array of `pass` / `fail` results, which is sent back to the caller
4. On `vote`: the post is embedded and stored in Qdrant along with the user's "good" or "slop" rating as metadata
5. Every new vote becomes in-context reference material for future classifications, so the filter gradually adapts to personal taste

## Customization

- **LLM** – swap the `OpenAI Chat Model` for any LangChain-compatible chat model (Claude, Ollama, etc.)
- **Prompt** – edit the system prompt inside the `Basic LLM Chain` node to match your own feed taste
- **Similarity threshold** – change the value in the `Filter` node (default `0.7`) to make RAG examples looser or stricter

## Compatibility

- n8n Version 2.17.0 or above
- Cloud or Self-Hosted
- Requires: OpenAI account, Qdrant instance

*Note: post contents sent through this workflow are forwarded to the configured LLM and embeddings provider (OpenAI by default). Swap those nodes for a local or alternative provider if that is a concern.*

parameters.content:
## How it works
A single webhook exposes two actions, selected by a `?action=` query parameter.
_Every vote is stored as an embedding so future classifications can reference it, letting the filter adapt to your taste over time._
1. A `Switch` routes requests based on the `action` parameter
2. On `analyze`: each post is enriched with similar prior-rated posts from Qdrant (RAG) and sent to the LLM with a strict quality-gate prompt
3. The LLM returns a JSON array of `pass` / `fail` results to the caller
4. On `vote`: the post is embedded and stored in Qdrant with the "good" or "slop" rating as metadata

Fallback: empty posts are skipped automatically.

## Setup
- [ ] Add your OpenAI credentials to the `OpenAI Chat Model` and `Embeddings OpenAI` nodes
- [ ] Add your Qdrant credentials to both `Qdrant Vector Store` nodes and create a collection named `stopslopin`
- [ ] Activate the workflow, copy the webhook URL, and paste it into the StopSlopIn Chrome extension settings

## Customization
- Swap the `OpenAI Chat Model` for any LangChain-compatible chat model (Claude, Ollama, etc.)
- Edit the system prompt inside the `Basic LLM Chain` node to match your taste
- Change the similarity threshold in the `Filter` node (default `0.7`)

messageValues.message:
=You are a **strict quality gate** for a LinkedIn feed. You receive a JSON array of posts (`id` + `text`) and classify each as "pass" or "fail".

**EXTREMELY IMPORTANT:** Only posts that clearly deliver real value to the reader may pass. Everything else is slop and must fail. This is not a fair/lenient filter — it is a hard gate. When in doubt, **fail**. The feed owner would rather lose a borderline post than see another piece of slop.

## Primary test

A post passes ONLY IF a reader would walk away having **learned something specific, seen something genuinely built, or gained a useful firsthand perspective**. If you cannot name what concrete value the post delivers in one sentence, fail it.

### What qualifies as quality content

- **Practical tips and tricks** — a concrete technique the reader can apply. Examples: "use a Redis flag with TTL to prevent overlapping n8n executions", "share one Execute Workflow Trigger with a switch node for multi-action sub-workflows", "how to use hooks in Claude Code to run tests on save".
- **Tutorials and how-tos** — written guide, video walkthrough, or step-by-step on using a tool, library, API, framework, or technique.
- **Builder showcase** — the author actually built something and demos what it does. Value stands even if every link were removed. An invitation to try it is fine; a pure pitch is not.
- **SaaS / product demo with substance** — founder explains a real problem and shows the product solving it. Not "limited spots, DM me".
- **Firsthand technical or industry commentary** — original analysis of tech, tools, workflows, or domain developments from someone who actually works in the space.
- **Genuine craft / career / psychology insight** — thoughtful perspective on learning, work, or self-development grounded in personal experience, not motivational-poster platitudes.
- **Short authentic milestones** — a brief, real update tied to something concrete the author did (e.g. "just got certified as an n8n Expert Partner 🎉"). Authentic tone, not engagement-farming.

Any post that does not clearly fit one of the above patterns is a fail candidate. A "nice observation" or "beautiful symbolism" does not qualify. Neither does a clickbait tease over mystery media.

## Hard fails (single signal → immediate fail)

Any ONE of these fails the post. Do not apply the 2+ rule here.

1. **Political or geopolitical content.** Partisan takes, nation-vs-nation framing, policy hot takes, elections, ideological manifestos, "country X is quietly doing Y" dramatization, unsourced geopolitical hype stats. Narrow exception only when a practitioner discusses a specific technical/regulatory detail they personally work with (e.g. a concrete GDPR implementation note in their own product).
2. **Forced-inspiration / motivational-poster content.** Mundane subject (supply chain, nature, sports, factory tour) + tacked-on "beautiful symbolism of vision/journey/ideas becoming reality" framing with no firsthand stake and no actionable takeaway. The banana-chip-factory-as-metaphor-for-entrepreneurship pattern.
3. **Pure engagement or lead-magnet funnels.** "Comment YES for the link", "drop 'X' and I'll DM you the RSVP", "repost to unlock part 2", "like for the template", "join the waitlist". The post's primary purpose is capturing comments/DMs/signups, not delivering value.
4. **Fabricated feel-good stories** with unrealistic neat outcomes ("helped a stranger → she was the CEO → got hired"). Performative-vulnerability-to-CTA pipelines.
5. **Hustle porn / thought-leader cosplay.** "I wake up at 4am", "That's what makes you a 50x builder", "Most people are thinking about X the wrong way" followed by generic common knowledge framed as proprietary insight.
6. **Humble brags framed as gratitude.** "I'm humbled/honored/thrilled to announce…" opener when the post is promotional or empty of substance. A short authentic milestone without the formula is fine.
7. **Off-topic hashtag farming.** 10+ hashtags at the bottom that are mostly unrelated to the post's actual topic (e.g. `#healthcare #education #leadership #inspiration #whatinspiresme` on a banana-chip factory post). This is reach-bait. Topical, relevant hashtag clusters are fine.

## Soft fails (2+ signals AND fails to clear the quality bar → fail)

If a post does not clearly deliver value AND hits two or more of the following, fail it.

**Structure**
- Broetry: one sentence per line, blank lines between, dramatic pacing for manufactured impact
- Fake dialogue: "Me: … Boss: … Me: …" scripts with tidy outcomes
- Generic numbered listicle: "10 things nobody tells you about X" where X is interchangeable
- Bolded inline headers with colon descriptions mimicking AI formatting (`**Key:** text`)
- Clickbait opener emoji + tease: "Did you know🤯", "Wait till you see this👀", "You won't believe😱"

**AI language**
- Clusters (3+) of buzzwords: delve, leverage, utilize, harness, foster, facilitate, empower, elevate, navigate, streamline, underscore, unleash, unlock, bolster, cultivate | pivotal, robust, seamless, groundbreaking, transformative, holistic, nuanced, multifaceted, cutting-edge, invaluable, paramount | landscape, realm, tapestry, synergy, testament, beacon, paradigm, ecosystem, cornerstone, catalyst, blueprint
- Em-dash abuse as universal punctuation (the "ChatGPT dash")
- Uniform sentence length with no natural short/long variation
- Zero typos or imperfections in a long, personal-sounding post
- Formal, emotionally detached tone reading like corporate documentation
- "It's not about X, it's about Y" parallelism, especially repeated
- Hedging overload: typically, might be, generally speaking, can also, to some extent
- Filler phrases: "it's important to note", "in today's rapidly evolving", "at the end of the day", "in the realm of", "that being said", "furthermore", "moreover"

**Content**
- Generic advice that applies to anyone — swap one noun and it fits any industry
- Motivational poster tone with no concrete data, names, dates, or verifiable detail
- Hot-take theater: "Unpopular opinion:" followed by an extremely popular opinion
- News repackaging as civilizational turning point with no firsthand analysis
- Unsourced big-number hype stats. Numbers only count toward quality when sourced, firsthand, or verifiable. Unsourced big-number claims count **against** the post, not for it.

**Promotional**
- Discount codes, "limited spots", "grab it now", "spots filling up" urgency
- Affiliate-style ad copy without genuine experience
- "Free PDF / template / checklist" when the post has no standalone value without it
- Entire structure is setup-to-CTA with no value before the CTA

**Formatting**
- **Decorative** emoji padding — sparkles, fire, rockets, flexed biceps sprinkled for vibes with no semantic role. Emoji that carry meaning (📌 note, ✅ solution, 🚩 warning, 💡 idea, ⚠️ caution, 👉 link pointer, ⏱️ timing) are fine and must NOT count against the post. Test: if removing the emoji loses information (category, sentiment, emphasis), it is meaningful.
- ALL CAPS lines used for artificial emphasis
- Hashtag stuffing **inline in prose**: country/topic hashtags used as nouns in sentences ("this week #Germany moving away from #USA"). A hashtag cluster at the bottom of the post is not the same thing and is fine when topical.
- Formatting inconsistency paired with grandiose claims: mixed bullet styles (`+`/`-`), typos alongside sweeping statements — signals careless LLM-assisted drafting

## Video and image posts

Many posts are a short caption over a video, image, or carousel you cannot see. Infer what the media likely contains from the caption:

- If the caption credibly signals substantive value — a demo of a tool the author built, a tutorial, a specific technique, a firsthand story — pass it.
- If the caption is just vibes, hashtags, and a clickbait hook ("A thing of beauty…", "Did you know🤯", "Pure inspiration 💪") with no indication the media teaches or shows anything specific, fail it. A mysterious video behind an empty caption is almost certainly not quality content.
- Never pass a post on the hope that "maybe the video is good". The caption must carry enough signal on its own.

## Prior ratings

Posts may include a `prior_ratings` array of similar human-voted examples, each with `text`, `vote` ("good" or "slop"), and `similarity` (0.0–1.0). Use as reference, not rules:

- High similarity (0.85+) with consistent votes (e.g. 3× slop) = strong signal.
- Low similarity (<0.75) or mixed votes = weak hint.
- Empty or missing = judge by the rules above alone.

Never override an obviously quality post because a vaguely similar one was voted slop, or vice versa. Prior ratings inform judgment; they do not replace it.

## Doubt → fail

If you cannot clearly name the concrete value a post provides in one sentence, fail it. A strict gate is the point. The feed owner will gain far more signal from rejecting borderline posts than from letting them through.

## Input

Each post has `id`, `text`, and optionally `prior_ratings`:

{posts_json}

## Output

Return ONLY a raw JSON array. No markdown fences, no explanation, no wrapping:

[{"id":"<post_id>","status":"pass"},{"id":"<post_id>","status":"fail"}]

One entry per input post. Exact same `id` from input. `status` is "pass" or "fail" only.

parameters.content:
## 1. Compare posts one by one with previous ones
Retrieve similar posts (with a certain relevance score) from the vector store and add them to the following LLM Analysis including the belonging rating done by you in the past.

## 2. Analyze posts and return rating
Take all posts (including additional rating context if available) and analyze them for quality. Return a JSON object containing the post ID and the final rating.

## Optional: Store manual rating for post
Save the manually voted post with it's contend and vote in a vector store.

body.text:
I had been meaning to share this earlier, but honestly, between deep dives into AI experimentation, building new automation systems, creating agentic workflows, and what I call my “healthy addiction” to n8n and AI innovation, time simply flew by.

When this package arrived, it felt special, not because it was just a gift, but because it represented something much bigger: the journey of building, teaching, and contributing to a global automation movement.

For me, N8N is not just another automation platform. It has become a major part of my professional ecosystem and creative journey.

Over the years, through my n8n training programs, I’ve had the privilege of reaching 31,631 students on Udemy, and across all my platforms including YouTube and other learning channels, that number is now approaching 100K learners worldwide.

What makes this even more exciting is the impact of my public n8n templates. Those templates have now crossed more than 500K reaches on the n8n template ecosystem. And that number only reflects direct visibility. When you consider how many users duplicated, customized, expanded, and redeployed those workflows into their own businesses, teams, and products, the real impact becomes exponentially larger.

That is the beauty of automation:
One shared workflow can multiply into thousands of transformed processes around the world.

As an official n8n #Ambassador in #Melbourne, being part of this ecosystem is deeply meaningful to me. I have watched n8n evolve into one of the most powerful workflow orchestration platforms in the world, and now with the rapid acceleration of AI, it is becoming even more extraordinary.

Today, n8n is no longer just automation.
It is becoming the backbone for intelligent AI agents, autonomous business operations, and next-generation workflow ecosystems.

Huge thanks to the entire n8n team for this thoughtful gesture, and special appreciation to Jan Oberhauser for building a platform that empowers creators, educators, developers, and innovators like us to create real impact.

This gift bag may look small, but to me it symbolizes a massive shared journey of innovation, learning, and global transformation.

And this is only the beginning.

Stay connected with me as I continue sharing advanced n8n workflows, AI agent systems, automation strategies, and future-ready business solutions through my courses and YouTube channel.

Start your n8n journey today using my link: https://n8n.syncbricks.com
My YouTube Channel: https://lnkd.in/dDfNXV_M
My Udemy Course : https://lnkd.in/diu6cVRQ
My n8n Creator Profile : https://lnkd.in/dWqk8Qbh

#n8n #automation #aiautomation #aiagents #workflowautomation #nocode #lowcode #digitaltransformation #udemyinstructor #contentcreator #n8nambassador #artificialintelligence 🚀
… more

user.bio: Workflow Optimization Expert | Software Architect. Use my link to book an initial consultation for custom built workflows using n8n.

data.details:
The IF node can be used to implement binary conditional logic in your workflow. You can set up one-to-many conditions to evaluate each item of data being inputted into the node. That data will either evaluate to TRUE or FALSE and route out of the node accordingly.

This node has multiple types of conditions: Bool, String, Number, and Date & Time.

data.details: The Filter node can be used to filter items based on a condition. If the condition is met, the item will be passed on to the next node. If the condition is not met, the item will be omitted. Conditions can be combined together by AND(meet all conditions), or OR(meet at least one condition).
